subject: Physics
q: An element $\Delta l=\Delta x \hat{i}$ is placed at the origin and carries a large current $I=10 \mathrm{~A}$. The magnetic field on the $y$-axis at a distance of $0.5 \mathrm{~m}$ from the elements $\Delta x$ of $1 \mathrm{~cm}$ length is:
question: An element $\Delta l=\Delta x \hat{i}$ is placed at the origin and carries a large current $I=10 \mathrm{~A}$. The magnetic field on the $y$-axis at a distance of $0.5 \mathrm{~m}$ from the elements $\Delta x$ of $1 \mathrm{~cm}$ length is:
solution: $\begin{aligned} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dB}} & =\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{I}}{4 \pi} \frac{(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{d} l} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}})}{\mathrm{r}^3}(\text { Tesla) } \\ & =\frac{10^{-7} \times 10 \times\left(\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{100}\right)(+\hat{\mathrm{k}})}{\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^3}=4 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~T}(+\hat{\mathrm{k}})\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
An electron is allowed to move with constant velocity along the axis of current carrying straight solenoid.
(A) The electron will experience magnetic force along the axis of the solenoid.
(B) The electron will not experience magnetic force.
(C) The electron will continue to move along the axis of the solenoid.
(D) The electron will be accelerated along the axis of the solenoid.
(E) The electron will follow parabolic path-inside the solenoid.
Choose the correct answer from the option given below:
question:
An electron is allowed to move with constant velocity along the axis of current carrying straight solenoid.
(A) The electron will experience magnetic force along the axis of the solenoid.
(B) The electron will not experience magnetic force.
(C) The electron will continue to move along the axis of the solenoid.
(D) The electron will be accelerated along the axis of the solenoid.
(E) The electron will follow parabolic path-inside the solenoid.
Choose the correct answer from the option given below:
solution:
The Lorentz force is given by F→=q(v→×B→)=qvBsinθn^. The angle θ=0°. Hence the force experienced is zero.

Thus, the electron will not experience any magnetic force and the electron will continue to move along the axis.

subject: Physics
q: A charge $Q$ is uniformly distributed over the surface of non conducting disc of radius $R$. The disc rotates about an axis perpendicular to its plane and passing through its centre with an angular velocity $\omega$. As a result of this rotation a magnetic field of induction $B$ is obtained at the centre of the disc. If we keep both the amount of charge placed on the disc and its angular velocity to be constant and vary the radius of the disc then the variation of the magnetic induction at the centre of the disc will be represented by the figure
question: A charge $Q$ is uniformly distributed over the surface of non conducting disc of radius $R$. The disc rotates about an axis perpendicular to its plane and passing through its centre with an angular velocity $\omega$. As a result of this rotation a magnetic field of induction $B$ is obtained at the centre of the disc. If we keep both the amount of charge placed on the disc and its angular velocity to be constant and vary the radius of the disc then the variation of the magnetic induction at the centre of the disc will be represented by the figure
solution: Consider ring like element of disc of radius $r$ and thickness $\mathrm{dr}$. If $\sigma$ is charge per unit area, then charge on the element $d q=\sigma(2 \pi r d r)$ current ' $i$ ' associated with rotating charge $\mathrm{dq}$ is $\mathrm{i}=\frac{(\mathrm{dq}) \mathrm{w}}{2 \pi}=\sigma \mathrm{wrdr}$ Magnetic field $\mathrm{dB}$ at center due to element $\mathrm{dB}=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{i}}{2 \mathrm{r}}=\frac{\mu_0 \sigma \omega \mathrm{dr}}{2}$ $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{B}_{\text {net }}=\int \mathrm{dB}=\frac{\mu_0 \sigma \omega}{2} \int_0^{\mathrm{R}} \mathrm{dr}=\frac{\mu_0 \sigma \omega \mathrm{R}}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{B}_{\text {net }}=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{Q} \omega}{2 \pi \mathrm{R}} \quad\left[\because \mathrm{Q}=\sigma \pi \mathrm{R}^2\right] \end{aligned}$ So if $Q$ and $w$ are unchanged then $\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{net}} \propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{R}}$ Hence variation of $B_{\text {net }}$ with $R$ should be a rectangular hyperbola as represented in (A).

subject: Physics
q: Consider two thin identical conducting wires covered with very thin insulating material. One of the wires is bent into a loop and produces magnetic field $\mathrm{B}_1$, at its centre when a current $\mathrm{I}$ passes through it. The ratio $\mathrm{B}_1: \mathrm{B}_2$ is:
question: Consider two thin identical conducting wires covered with very thin insulating material. One of the wires is bent into a loop and produces magnetic field $\mathrm{B}_1$, at its centre when a current $\mathrm{I}$ passes through it. The ratio $\mathrm{B}_1: \mathrm{B}_2$ is:
solution:
For loop $B=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{nI}}{2 \mathrm{a}}$ where, $a$ is the radius of loop.
Then, $B_1=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 a}$
Now, for coil $\mathrm{B}=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{I}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{2 \mathrm{nA}}{\mathrm{x}^3}$ at the centre $x=$ radius of loop
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{B}_2 &=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{2 \times 3 \times(\mathrm{I} / 3) \times \pi(\mathrm{a} / 3)^2}{(\mathrm{a} / 3)^3} \\
&=\frac{\mu_0 \cdot 3 \mathrm{I}}{2 \mathrm{a}} \\
\therefore & \frac{\mathrm{B}_1}{\mathrm{~B}_2}=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{I} / 2 \mathrm{a}}{\mu_0 \cdot 3 \mathrm{I} / 2 \mathrm{a}} \\
\mathrm{B}_1: \mathrm{B}_2=1: 3
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
q: An electron is moving along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi></math> direction with a velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>6</mn></msup><msup><mi>ms</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. It enters a region of uniform electric field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi>V</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>c</mi><mi>m</mi></math> pointing along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi></math> direction. The magnitude and direction of the magnetic field set up in this region such that the electron keeps moving along the x direction will be:
question: An electron is moving along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi></math> direction with a velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>6</mn></msup><msup><mi>ms</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. It enters a region of uniform electric field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi>V</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>c</mi><mi>m</mi></math> pointing along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi></math> direction. The magnitude and direction of the magnetic field set up in this region such that the electron keeps moving along the x direction will be:
solution:
q∨B=qE

B=EV=300006×106=5×103T

Magnetic force F=q(V→×B→)

So Ans.5×103T ,along +Z direction

subject: Physics
q: A uniform magnetic field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.3</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> is along the positive <math> <mtext>Z</mtext> </math> -direction. A rectangular loop (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mi>b</mi><mi>c</mi><mi>d</mi></math>) of sides <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>cm</mi><mo>×</mo><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>cm</mi></math> carries a current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>. Out of the following different orientations which one corresponds to stable equilibrium?
question: A uniform magnetic field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.3</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> is along the positive <math> <mtext>Z</mtext> </math> -direction. A rectangular loop (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mi>b</mi><mi>c</mi><mi>d</mi></math>) of sides <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>cm</mi><mo>×</mo><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>cm</mi></math> carries a current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>. Out of the following different orientations which one corresponds to stable equilibrium?
solution:
U=-M→·B→=-MBcosθ

For stable equilibrium potential energy should be minimum and τ=0

Umin=-MB    when θ=0°

⇒cos⁡θ = 1

⇒M→ and B→  are on same direction.

subject: Physics
q: A particle of charge $-16 \times 10^{-18}$ coulomb moving with velocity $10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ along the $\mathrm{x}$-axis enters a region where a magnetic field of induction $\mathrm{B}$ is along the $\mathrm{y}$-axis, and an electric field of magnitude $10^4 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}$ is along the negative z-axis. If the charged particle continues moving along the $\mathrm{x}$-axis, the magnitude of $\mathrm{B}$ is
question: A particle of charge $-16 \times 10^{-18}$ coulomb moving with velocity $10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ along the $\mathrm{x}$-axis enters a region where a magnetic field of induction $\mathrm{B}$ is along the $\mathrm{y}$-axis, and an electric field of magnitude $10^4 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}$ is along the negative z-axis. If the charged particle continues moving along the $\mathrm{x}$-axis, the magnitude of $\mathrm{B}$ is
solution: Since there is no deviation in the path of the charged particle, so net force due to presence of electric and magnetic field must be zero $\Rightarrow \mathrm{vB}=\mathrm{qE} \Rightarrow \mathrm{B}=\frac{\mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{V}}=\frac{10^4}{10}=10^3 \mathrm{~Wb} / \mathrm{m}^2$

subject: Physics
q: A solenoid of length $0.5 \mathrm{~m}$ has a radius of $1 \mathrm{~cm}$ and is made up of ' $\mathrm{m}$ ' number of turns. It carries a current of $5 \mathrm{~A}$. If the magnitude of the magnetic field inside the solenoid is $6.28 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~T}$ then the value of $m$ is _____.
question: A solenoid of length $0.5 \mathrm{~m}$ has a radius of $1 \mathrm{~cm}$ and is made up of ' $\mathrm{m}$ ' number of turns. It carries a current of $5 \mathrm{~A}$. If the magnitude of the magnetic field inside the solenoid is $6.28 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~T}$ then the value of $m$ is _____.
solution:
$\mu_0 n i=B \quad n=$ number of turns per unit length
$\mu_0\left(\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\ell}\right) \mathrm{i}=\mathrm{B}$
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{m}=\frac{B . \ell}{\mu_0 \mathrm{i}}=\frac{6.28 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5}{12.56 \times 10^{-7} \times 5} \\ & \mathrm{~m}=500\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A magnetic dipole experiences a torque of $80 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{~m}$ when placed in uniform magnetic field in such a way that dipole moment makes angle of $60^{\circ}$ with magnetic field. The potential energy of the dipole is :
question: A magnetic dipole experiences a torque of $80 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{~m}$ when placed in uniform magnetic field in such a way that dipole moment makes angle of $60^{\circ}$ with magnetic field. The potential energy of the dipole is :
solution:
$\tau=\mathrm{M} \times \mathrm{B}=\mathrm{MB} \sin 60=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \mathrm{MB}=80 \sqrt{3}$
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{MB}=160 \\ & \mathrm{U}=-\mathrm{M} \cdot \mathrm{B}=-\mathrm{MB} \cos 60 \\ & \mathrm{U}=-160 \times 1 / 2=-80 \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A coil having 100 turns, area of $5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}^2$, carrying current of $1 \mathrm{~mA}$ is placed in uniform magnetic field of $0.20 \mathrm{~T}$ such a way that plane of coil is perpendicular to the magnetic field. The work done in turning the coil through $90^{\circ}$ is _______ $\mu \mathrm{J}$.
question: A coil having 100 turns, area of $5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}^2$, carrying current of $1 \mathrm{~mA}$ is placed in uniform magnetic field of $0.20 \mathrm{~T}$ such a way that plane of coil is perpendicular to the magnetic field. The work done in turning the coil through $90^{\circ}$ is _______ $\mu \mathrm{J}$.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & W=\Delta U=U_f-U_i \\ & W=(-\vec{\mu} . \vec{B})_f-(-\vec{\mu} . \vec{B})_i \\ & =0+(\vec{\mu} \cdot \vec{B})_i \\ & =\left(100 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 1 \times 10^{-3}\right) \times 0.2 \mathrm{~J} \\ & =1 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~J}=100 \mu \mathrm{J}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
The magnitude of magnetic induction at mid-point O due to current arrangement as shown in figure will be

.
question:
The magnitude of magnetic induction at mid-point O due to current arrangement as shown in figure will be

.
solution:
The magnetic field at point O due to the two semi-infinite portions of both wires lying along line BE will be 0.

Using right-hand rule, it can be determined that the magnetic field due to both the semi-infinite portions of wires BC and ET which are perpendicular to BE will be outwards from the plane.

The magnetic field due to one such portion will be:

B=μ0I4πa2

Since the magnetic field of both the portions is in same direction, the net magnetic field at O will be:

Bnet=2×μ0I4πa2=μ0Iπa

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements : One is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).

Assertion (A) : In an uniform magnetic field, speed and energy remains the same for a moving charged particle.

Reason (R): Moving charged particle experiences magnetic force perpendicular to its direction of motion.
question:
Given below are two statements : One is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).

Assertion (A) : In an uniform magnetic field, speed and energy remains the same for a moving charged particle.

Reason (R): Moving charged particle experiences magnetic force perpendicular to its direction of motion.
options: Both Assertion and Reason are true and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true but Reason is NOT the correct explanation of Assertion.
Assertion is true but Reason is false.
Assertion is false but Reason is true.
solution:
The force experienced by the charge particle is perpendicular to the instantaneous velocity at all instants as F→=qv→×B→.

Hence, the magnetic force cannot bring any change in the speed of the charged particle. Since, the speed remains constant, the kinetic energy also remains constant.

subject: Physics
q: A very long solenoid of radius <math><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is carrying current <math><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">α</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow></msup><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> as a function of time <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mo>≥</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>.</mo></math> Counterclockwise current is taken to be positive. A circular conducting coil of radius <math><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is placed in the equitorial plane of the solenoid and concentric with the solenoid. The current induced in the outer coil is correctly depicted, as a function of time, by:
question: A very long solenoid of radius <math><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is carrying current <math><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">α</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow></msup><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> as a function of time <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mo>≥</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>.</mo></math> Counterclockwise current is taken to be positive. A circular conducting coil of radius <math><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is placed in the equitorial plane of the solenoid and concentric with the solenoid. The current induced in the outer coil is correctly depicted, as a function of time, by:
solution:
ϕouter=μ0nKte-αt4πR2
ε=-dϕdt=-μ0nK4πR2(e-αt1-αt)
iinduced=-ce-αt1-αtResistance
At t=0, Iinduced=-ve
From the above equation and initial value of current we can say the correct graph is B.

subject: Physics
q:
As shown in the figure, a current of 2 A flowing in an equilateral triangle of side 43 cm . The magnetic field at the centroid O of the triangle is :

(Neglect the effect of earth’s magnetic field)
question:
As shown in the figure, a current of 2 A flowing in an equilateral triangle of side 43 cm . The magnetic field at the centroid O of the triangle is :

(Neglect the effect of earth’s magnetic field)
solution:
Perpendicular distance of centroid from any of the sides will be, dtan60°=23

⇒d=2 cm

Each side will subtend an angle of 120° at the centre. Also magnetic field due to all sides will be out of plane of paper, hence total magnetic field can be written as

B=3×μ0i4πdsin60°+sin60°

=3×10-7×22×10-2×3

=33×10-5 T

subject: Physics
q: A straight wire AB of mass 40 g and length 50 cm is suspended by a pair of flexible leads in uniform magnetic field of magnitude 0.40 T as shown in the figure. The magnitude of the current required in the wire to remove the tension in the supporting leads is _____ A. (Take g=10 m s-2.
question: A straight wire AB of mass 40 g and length 50 cm is suspended by a pair of flexible leads in uniform magnetic field of magnitude 0.40 T as shown in the figure. The magnitude of the current required in the wire to remove the tension in the supporting leads is _____ A. (Take g=10 m s-2.
solution:
The formula to calculate the magnetic force on the conductor is given by

FB= ILB   ...1

The formula to calculate the gravitational force on the conductor is given by

FG= mg   ...2

Equate both the equations and simplify to obtain the required current through the conductor.

ILB=mg⇒I= mgLB   ...3

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (3) to calculate the required current through the conductor.

I=401000 kg×10 m s-20.5 m×0.40 T=2 A

subject: Physics
q: Two magnetic dipoles X and Y are placed at a separation d , with their axes perpendicular to each other. The dipole moment of Y is twice that of X . A particle of charge q is passing through their mid-point P , at angle θ=45o with the horizontal line, as shown in figure. What would be the magnitude of force on the particle at that instant? ( d is much larger than the dimension of the dipole)
question: Two magnetic dipoles X and Y are placed at a separation d , with their axes perpendicular to each other. The dipole moment of Y is twice that of X . A particle of charge q is passing through their mid-point P , at angle θ=45o with the horizontal line, as shown in figure. What would be the magnitude of force on the particle at that instant? ( d is much larger than the dimension of the dipole)
solution:
BX=2μ04πMd/2 along x -axis
BY=μ04π2Md/2 along y -axis
BX=BY

Hence Bnet will be at 45o from x -axis.
Now, velocity and magnetic field are in same direction. Hence no force will act on the particle.

subject: Physics
q: As shown in the figure, two infinitely long, identical wires are bent by 90o and placed in such a way that the segments LP and QM are along the x - axis, while segments PS and QN are parallel to the y - axis. If OP=OQ=4cm, and the magnitude of the magnetic field at O is 10-4 T, and the two wires carry equal currents (see figure), the magnitude of the current in each wire and the direction of the magnetic field at O will be μ0=4π×10-7NA-2:
question: As shown in the figure, two infinitely long, identical wires are bent by 90o and placed in such a way that the segments LP and QM are along the x - axis, while segments PS and QN are parallel to the y - axis. If OP=OQ=4cm, and the magnitude of the magnetic field at O is 10-4 T, and the two wires carry equal currents (see figure), the magnitude of the current in each wire and the direction of the magnetic field at O will be μ0=4π×10-7NA-2:
solution:
As we know that the magnetic field due to a infinite wire at a distance of d is given as 

B=μ0i4πd

but there are two wires so that the net magnetic field is given as

B=2μ0i4πd

10-4=24π×10-7×i4π×4100

i=20A

question:
A current of 1.5 A is flowing through a triangle, of side 9 cm each. The magnetic field at the centroid of the triangle is :
(Assume that the current is flowing in the clockwise direction.)
solution:
B1=μ0I4πR2sin60=4π×10-7×1.54π.332×10-2×2×32=1×10-5
Bnet=3B1=3×10-5T⊗

subject: Physics
q: An iron rod of volume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> and relative permeability <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1000</mn></math> is placed as core in a solenoid with<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>turns</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> . If a current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is passed through the solenoid, then the magnetic moment of the rod will be :
question: An iron rod of volume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> and relative permeability <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1000</mn></math> is placed as core in a solenoid with<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>turns</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> . If a current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is passed through the solenoid, then the magnetic moment of the rod will be :
solution:
Given,

Volume of iron rod is, V=10-3 m3

Relative permeability, μr=1000

No of turns per unit length of solenoid is, n=Nl=10 turns cm-1=1000 turns m-1

Current through the solenoid is, i=0.5 A

Magnetic moment of an iron core solenoid is given by,

M=(μr-1) NiA

⇒M=μr-1NiVl=μr-1NliV

⇒M=1000-1×1000×0.5×10-3

⇒M=499.5≈500 A m2

subject: Physics
q: Two concentric coils each of radius equal to $2 \pi \mathrm{cm}$ are placed at right angles to each other. 3 Ampere and 4 ampere are the currents flowing in each coil respectively. The magnetic induction in Weber $/ \mathrm{m}^2$ at the centre of the coils will be $\left(\mu_0=4 \pi \times 10^{-7}\right.$ Wb/A-m)
question: Two concentric coils each of radius equal to $2 \pi \mathrm{cm}$ are placed at right angles to each other. 3 Ampere and 4 ampere are the currents flowing in each coil respectively. The magnetic induction in Weber $/ \mathrm{m}^2$ at the centre of the coils will be $\left(\mu_0=4 \pi \times 10^{-7}\right.$ Wb/A-m)
solution:
$B=\frac{\mu_o}{2 r} \sqrt{I_1^2+I_2^2}$
$B=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7}}{2 \times 2 \pi \times 10^{-2}} \times 5$
$B=5 \times 10^{-5}$

subject: Physics
q: A beam of protons with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> enters a uniform magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> at an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> to the magnetic field, the pitch of the resulting helical path of protons is close to : (Mass of the proton<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>67</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>27</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>, charge of the proton<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>69</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>19</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>)
question: A beam of protons with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> enters a uniform magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> at an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> to the magnetic field, the pitch of the resulting helical path of protons is close to : (Mass of the proton<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>67</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>27</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>, charge of the proton<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>69</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>19</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>)
solution:
Pitch

=(Vcosθ)T

=(Vcosθ)2πmeB

=4×105cos60°2π0.3×101.67×10-271.69×1019

4 cm

subject: Physics
q: Two particles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi></math> having equal charges are being accelerated through the same potential difference. Thereafter, they enter normally in a region of uniform magnetic field and describes circular paths of radii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> respectively. The mass ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi></math> is :
question: Two particles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi></math> having equal charges are being accelerated through the same potential difference. Thereafter, they enter normally in a region of uniform magnetic field and describes circular paths of radii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> respectively. The mass ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi></math> is :
solution:
Energy gained due to potential difference by charged particle:

KE=qV

Relation between momentum & kinetic energy:

p=2mKE

Now, radius of the circular path under magnetic field will be given by, 

R=mvqB=pqB=2m(KE)qB=2mqVqB

Therefore,

R∝m⇒m∝R2

⇒m1 m2=R1R22

subject: Physics
q: Two insulated circular loop A and B radius a carrying a current of I in the anti clockwise direction as shown in figure. The magnitude of the magnetic induction at the centre will be:
question: Two insulated circular loop A and B radius a carrying a current of I in the anti clockwise direction as shown in figure. The magnitude of the magnetic induction at the centre will be:
solution:
Right hand thumb rule states that, “If you imagine holding a current carrying wire in your right-hand with your thumb pointing towards the direction of electric current flow then the direction in which your fingers curl, gives the direction of lines of force of the magnetic field”. In this case, direciton of magnetic field at the centre for both wires is as shown below.

Magnetic field due to a circular wire is given by, BA=BB=μ0I2a

As both magnetic field lines at centre are perpendicular, therefore

∵Bnet=BA2+BB2=2μ0I2a=μ0I2a

subject: Physics
q: A long straight wire of a circular cross-section with radius ' $a$ ' carries a steady current $I$. The current I is uniformly distributed across this cross-section. The plot of magnitude of magnetic field B with distance $r$ from the centre of the wire is given by
question: A long straight wire of a circular cross-section with radius ' $a$ ' carries a steady current $I$. The current I is uniformly distributed across this cross-section. The plot of magnitude of magnetic field B with distance $r$ from the centre of the wire is given by
solution: We know inside the wire $\begin{array}{ll} B=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi R^2} \cdot r & (O < r < R) \\ \text { And } B=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi r} & \text { for }(R < r) \end{array}$

subject: Physics
q: Magnetic fields at two points on the axis of a circular coil at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>05</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> from the centre are in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>1</mn></math>. The radius of coil is _______ .
question: Magnetic fields at two points on the axis of a circular coil at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>05</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> from the centre are in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>1</mn></math>. The radius of coil is _______ .
solution:
We know, the magnetic field on the axis of a current carrying circular ring is given by

B=μ04π2NIAR2+x23/2

∴  B1 B2=81=R2+0.22R2+0.0523/2

4R2+0.052=R2+0.22

4R2-R2=0.22-4×0.052

4R2-R2=0.22-0.12

3R2=0.3×0.1

R2=0.12⇒R=0.1

subject: Physics
q: Two identical circular loops $P$ and $Q$ each of radius $r$ are lying in parallel planes such that they have common axis. The current through $P$ and $Q$ are $I$ and $4 I$ respectively in clockwise direction as seen from $O$. The net magnetic field at $O$ is:
question: Two identical circular loops $P$ and $Q$ each of radius $r$ are lying in parallel planes such that they have common axis. The current through $P$ and $Q$ are $I$ and $4 I$ respectively in clockwise direction as seen from $O$. The net magnetic field at $O$ is:
solution:
Two identical circular loops P and Q, each of radius $r$, are separated by distance $r$ along their common axis. Current in P is $I$ and in Q is $4I$ (both clockwise as seen from O).
Point O is at the midpoint. The magnetic fields from each loop at O:
$B_1 = \frac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2(R^2 + R^2)^{3/2}}$ (from P, pointing toward Q)
$B_2 = \frac{4\mu_0 I R^2}{2(R^2 + R^2)^{3/2}}$ (from Q, pointing toward P)
$B_{net} = B_2 - B_1 = \frac{3\mu_0 I R^2}{2(2R^2)^{3/2}} = \frac{3\mu_0 I}{4\sqrt{2}R}$
Direction: towards Q.

subject: Physics
q: A single turn current loop in the shape of a right angle triangle with sides <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>13</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is carrying a current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>. The loop is in a uniform magnetic field of magnitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>75</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> whose direction is parallel to the current in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>13</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> side of the loop. The magnitude of the magnetic force on the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> side will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mn>130</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _______.
question: A single turn current loop in the shape of a right angle triangle with sides <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>13</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is carrying a current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>. The loop is in a uniform magnetic field of magnitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>75</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> whose direction is parallel to the current in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>13</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> side of the loop. The magnitude of the magnetic force on the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> side will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mn>130</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _______.
solution:
Here, B=0.75 T

The magnitude of magnetic force on the 5 cm side is FAB=i×lABBsinθ           angle between AC & AB

=2×5100×0.75×1213

=9130 N

Hence, the value of x=9.

subject: Physics
q: An infinite wire has a circular bend of radius a, and carrying a current I as shown in figure. The magnitude of magnetic field at the origin $O$ of the arc is given by :
question: An infinite wire has a circular bend of radius a, and carrying a current I as shown in figure. The magnitude of magnetic field at the origin $O$ of the arc is given by :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{B}_1=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{i}}{4 \pi \mathrm{a}} \otimes \\ & \mathrm{B}_2=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{\mathrm{i}}{\mathrm{a}}\left(\frac{3 \pi}{2}\right) \otimes \\ & \mathrm{B}_3=0 \\ & \mathrm{~B}=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{\mathrm{i}}{\mathrm{a}}\left(1 \frac{3 \pi}{2}\right) \otimes\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Two identical wires <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math>, each of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>l</mi></math>, carry the same current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> . Wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is bent into a circle of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> is bent to form a square of side <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></msub></math> are the values of magnetic field at the centres of the circle and square respectively, then the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></msub><msub><mi>B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></msub></mfrac></math> is
question: Two identical wires <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math>, each of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>l</mi></math>, carry the same current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> . Wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is bent into a circle of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> is bent to form a square of side <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></msub></math> are the values of magnetic field at the centres of the circle and square respectively, then the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></msub><msub><mi>B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></msub></mfrac></math> is
solution:
As length of both the wires is same, 2πR=4a

⇒aR=2π4

⇒aR=π2

BA=μ0i2R and 

BB=μ0iπa22

Therefore, ratio will be BABB=μ0i2R×πa22 μ0i

=πa42R=π42π2=π282

subject: Physics
q: A charge particle moves along circular path in a uniform magnetic field in a cyclotron. The kinetic energy of the charge particle increases to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> times its initial value. What will be the ratio of new radius to the original radius of circular path of the charge particle?
question: A charge particle moves along circular path in a uniform magnetic field in a cyclotron. The kinetic energy of the charge particle increases to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> times its initial value. What will be the ratio of new radius to the original radius of circular path of the charge particle?
solution:
Radius of a charged particle moving in a magnetic field perpendicular to its velocity is given by, R=mvqB.

mv can be written in terms of kinetic energy as mv=2mKE. Therefore,

R=mvqB=2mKEqB

⇒R∝KE⇒R2R1=K2K1=41=21
